findings.description: parse_iso_date is documented as strict but strptime's %m/%d patterns accept non-zero-padded components (e.g. '2024-1-2' and even '2024-01- 2' parse successfully), so non-canonical YYYY-MM-DD inputs are silently accepted.
end_of_day_exclusive(date.max) raises OverflowError because adding one day exceeds datetime.max, an undocumented failure mode at the calendar boundary.
int() truncates toward zero, so a moment less than one second in the future (delta -0.5s -> 0) reports 'just now' instead of 'in the future'.
The awareness guard only checks tzinfo is None, so a pathological tzinfo whose utcoffset() returns None passes the check and the subtraction raises TypeError instead of the intended ValueError.
Because datetime subclasses date, a datetime argument satisfies the annotation and its time/timezone are silently discarded, which can mask caller mistakes.
business_days_between materializes every date in the span via day_range (O(n) time and memory) although a closed-form weekday count is O(1); only matters for very wide ranges.
day_range eagerly builds the full list (millions of entries for multi-millennium spans); a generator would bound memory, though returning a list is a defensible API choice.
datetime.strptime is substantially slower than date.fromisoformat for fixed ISO dates, which could matter in hot parsing paths.
Module docstring advertises 'pure functions' but utc_now (and humanize_delta's default reference) read the wall clock, so the claim is inaccurate.
is_weekend is the only public helper without a docstring.
The divmod remainders assigned on lines 61/64/67/70 (seconds/minutes/hours/days) are dead stores in their final branches; floor division would state intent more directly.
Durations cap at weeks, so year-old moments render as e.g. '104 w ago'; a month/year tier may read better, though the docstring does say 'coarse'.
PEP 604 'datetime | None' (and list[date] on line 32) require Python 3.10+/3.9+, relevant only if older interpreters must be supported.
